data.title: ClearBridge Canadian Equity Strategy Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
By Garey J. Aitken, CFA & Timothy W. Caulfield, CFA

Metals, Defensives Support Canadian Resilience

Market Overview

Despite the first quarter of 2025 being dominated by uncertainty surrounding U.S. tariffs, a battle-hardened Canadian equity market, highlighted by sparkling performance in the materials sector, finished the quarter in positive territory, with the S&P/TSX Composite Total Return Index (TRI) increasing 1.5%. This was quite the achievement for the primary bourse of a country that’s been subject to a steady diet of threats by the current U.S. administration, including not only continuously changing tariffs (and vows to “crush” and “permanently shut down” the Canadian auto industry), but also Canada’s sovereignty. But make no mistake, the prospects of a global trade war weighed heavily on sentiment and most sectors were worse off for it. After the Canadian equity market reached an all-time high on January 30, it subsequently declined in both February and March while credit spreads meaningfully increased for the first time since 2022.

Tariffs and related measures are expected to slow the pace of economic activity — consumer spending, business activity, hiring and investing — while concurrently stoking inflation from higher costs. The economic impact for Canada could potentially be severe depending on the scope and duration of the trade war. The frustration of market participants around the world, including in the U.S. itself, was arguably predictable and exacerbated by the “self-infliction” of such heavy-handed policy. Despite strong opposition from many corners, tariffs have gone ahead all the same.

With outsize performance in materials, investors were reminded of the unique significance of the gold and silver sub-industries in the overall Canadian benchmark, accounting for a combined weighting of approximately 10% as of quarter end. These securities’ “safe haven” status and low correlation to outcomes for the broader market provided significant ballast in the first quarter in contrast to more cyclical and growth-oriented sectors. The benchmark heavy gold sub-industry advanced more than 34%, helped by a gold price increase of more than 18% to finish the quarter at an all-time high of US$3,123 per ounce.

Benchmark 10-year interest rates in Canada and the U.S. declined 26 basis points and 36 bps to finish at 2.97% and 4.21%, respectively. While the Federal Reserve stood pat in the first quarter, the Bank of Canada cut rates for the sixth and seventh consecutive times in January and March, ultimately to 2.75%, intending to boost consumption and housing. The outlook for further cuts is complicated by inflation concerns tied to tariffs. The Canadian dollar was flat against the U.S. dollar, ending the quarter at $1.44 CDN/USD.

Exhibit 1: Precious Metals, Commodities Lead Market Performance

On an absolute return basis, the Canadian equity market’s advance was narrow, with only four of 11 sectors producing gains: materials, utilities, energy and communication services. The energy sector was broadly solid, with crude oil prices relatively flat ending the quarter at US$71.48/bbl (West Texas Intermediate) and NYMEX natural gas prices increasing 13% to US$4.12/mmbtu (NYMEX). Resilience was shown by both the predictable utilities and washed-out communication services sectors. Health care and information technology (IT) were the worst-performing sectors.

Performance Overview

The ClearBridge Canadian Equity Strategy outperformed its benchmark in the first quarter with positive security selection more than offsetting weak sector allocation. An underweight to the exceptionally strong materials sector detracted, partially offset by an underweight to the underperforming financials and IT sectors, as well as our large overweight to the outperforming utilities sector. Security selection was positive and strongest in utilities, energy and financials.

In materials, underlying commodity price strength led to strong returns for Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM) and Franco-Nevada (FNV), which the Strategy owns at an underweight to the benchmark.

In financials, Toronto-Dominion Bank (TD), the Strategy’s largest holding, materially outperformed the sector and all other banks, as its share price recovered from relative weakness in 2024 on anti-money-laundering fines, regulatory monitoring and asset restrictions. In addition to accelerating its CEO transition in January, TD announced in February that it would sell its entire equity investment in Charles Schwab and use the proceeds to buy back up to 5.7% of its outstanding shares, add over 100 bps to its already strong CET1 capital ratio, and invest in the business to drive organic growth.

In utilities, a large overweight to the sector and relative strength within it contributed to performance with the strength and predictability of regulated utility returns increasingly attractive in an uncertain environment. Utilities have benefited from lower interest rates, but their predictability seems equally, or more, important in the current environment.

Portfolio Positioning

With increased volatility and pockets of strength, trading activity in the first quarter of 2025 was more surgical, trimming select holdings on strength including a few defensive and more interest-rate-sensitive holdings such as Enbridge (ENB), Waste Connections (WCN), Metro (OTCPK:MTRAF)(OTCPK:MTTWF), ATCO (OTCPK:ACLLF), Hydro One (OTCPK:HRNNF) and Fortis (FTS). We also trimmed a few of the market’s deemed cyclical winners, such as Atkins-Realis (OTCPK:SNCAF), Manulife (MFC) and TMX Group (OTCPK:TMXXF). Meanwhile, buying centered around out-of-favor cyclical names Alimentation Couche-Tard (OTCPK:ANCTF), Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ), Canadian National Railway (CNI) as well as BCE (BCE) and Shopify (SHOP). Additions also included two new holdings: Teck Resources (TECK) and Intact Financial (IFC)(OTCPK:IFCZF). While the Strategy is typically low turnover, we will continue to be decisive when warranted and as further opportunities arise.

Recent weakness in the shares offered an attractive opportunity to buy Teck Resources in materials. In recent years, Teck has completed a major transformation into a pure-play copper growth company, having disposed of its oil sands and coal businesses in 2023 and 2024, respectively. Following these dispositions, Teck has an exceptionally strong financial position with ample cash on the balance sheet and is in the midst of returning meaningful capital to shareholders. Having completed the construction of its (60%-owned) giant Quebrada Blanca (QB) copper mine in 2024, Teck is focused on growing its copper production meaningfully in the coming years. Free cash flow is inflecting positively as QB kicks in. While demand is being impacted by geopolitical and trade tensions in the world, persistent supply challenges also characterize copper market fundamentals, to Teck’s benefit.

New addition IFC has illustrated its past success with its 20% market share in Canada, an average return on equity outperformance of 500+ bps versus the industry and high-single-digit compounding of profitability over the long term. IFC’s strengths are rooted in superior pricing and risk selection, claims expertise, a solid M&A track record and strong investment management contributions.

In IT, we continued to build our position in Shopify as recent results continue to illuminate the improved financial stability and capital allocation mindset guiding the business. The ever-expanding total addressable market, robust runway of embedded margin expansion and pricing power, improved capital allocation, and a best-in-class balance sheet continue to unlock the intrinsic value of the business at a rapid clip.

At quarter end, the Strategy’s largest sector exposures were financials, industrials and energy. Relative to the benchmark, the Strategy is overweight the generally defensive/non-cyclical consumer staples and utilities sectors, as well as industrials. The Strategy is most underweight the typically value-oriented/cyclical financials, materials and energy sectors.

Outlook

For Canada, concerns surrounding the likes of housing affordability and improvements in productivity have expanded to include the scope, magnitude and duration of tariffs and a host of related issues — for instance, diversifying export markets, including for Canadian energy. Developments of recent months are undoubtedly a net negative for the economy with “better-than-feared” tariff treatment by the U.S. administration so far the consolation prize. Canadian business and consumer confidence are weak. For business investment in Canada this could be setting up to be a dark period as commitments to long-term capital allocation decisions could be deemed too difficult to make.

For investors in Canadian equities, which in many cases have reach well beyond Canada’s borders, the overall outlook has been muddied. Global economic uncertainty has materially increased, new downside risks have emerged, and the rationale for the U.S. administration plunging the world into a trade war is at best unclear at the outset. In managing the ClearBridge Canadian Equity Strategy, we are always working with different degrees of imperfect information about the future; however, at present both transparency and visibility are lacking. This unsurprisingly leads to risk aversion in equities. Hopes that haphazard policies in the U.S. would ultimately wind up being more hyperbole and negotiating tactics have been dashed for the time being.

Through periods of turmoil, such as the one we are currently navigating, it is particularly important to maintain a long-term perspective, resisting myopia in the moment. For instance, Canada has a comparative advantage in the production of raw materials, including energy, and this has not changed. Canada is the number one export market for 34 U.S. states, often with deeply integrated ties.

Furthermore, the interconnectedness of the two countries is even more evident when considering that much of this trade is between parties with cross ownership. Global impacts and second-order effects will be critical. However, economically destructive policies at minimum provide strong incentives to find a resolution. And while cooperation is likely too valuable to ignore over time, tariffs and retaliatory measures will likely result in higher near-term inflation, inflicting a heavy “tax” as businesses make suboptimal decisions. Make no mistake; some damage is done. However, Canada’s economy is, and will remain, heavily intertwined with the U.S., as will many of the businesses owned in the Strategy.

Without compromising on valuation, owning businesses with resilience and predictability remains our preferred positioning. We like these businesses along with those facing challenges and uncertainties where a lower share price is already discounting the increased risk in the environment. When risks are heightened, probabilistic thinking becomes even more valuable in evaluating the range and likelihood of various future scenarios, directly impacting risk/reward assessments. Typical concurrent volatility in these circumstances can present exceptional opportunities. Our positioning can provide ballast in more challenging equity market environments, allows the Strategy to power ahead with predictable growth, and serves as dry powder when better risk/reward opportunities arise.

Portfolio Highlights

During the first quarter, the ClearBridge Canadian Equity Strategy outperformed its S&P/TSX benchmark. On an absolute basis, the Strategy generated gains in five of the 10 sectors in which it was invested (out of 11 total). The primary contributors were the materials, energy and utilities sectors while the main detractor was the IT sector.

Relative to the benchmark, security selection was positive and strongest in utilities, energy and financials, more than offsetting negative sector allocation. An underweight to the exceptionally strong materials sector detracted, partially offset by an underweight to the underperforming financials and IT sectors as well as our large overweight to the outperforming utilities sector.

On an individual stock basis, leading absolute contributors included Franco-Nevada, Toronto-Dominion Bank, Agnico Eagle Mines, Fortis and Waste Connections. Top absolute detractors included Alimentation Couche-Tard, Brookfield Corporation (BN), Bank of Nova Scotia (BNS), Royal Bank of Canada (RY) and Shopify.

Garey J. Aitken, CFA , Head of Canadian Equities

Timothy W. Caulfield, CFA, Dir. Canadian Eq. Research

data.title: ClearBridge Canadian Small Cap Strategy Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
By Garey J. Aitken, CFA & Michael Richmond, CFA

Gold Rush Carries Small Caps

Market Overview

We suspect market participants from a century ago would have felt right at home with the topics that dominated Canadian small cap equities in the first quarter of 2025 — tariffs and gold. Tariffs were by far the most impactful macro driver of equity performance. The “will-they, won’t-they” gyrations out of Washington resulted in a sell first, ask questions later approach to a broad swath of the Canadian small cap market.

Equities with even the faintest ties to either U.S. trade or the broader North American economy sold off during the quarter. The cyclically exposed small cap information technology (IT), industrials, financials and energy sectors all trailed the S&P/TSX Small Cap Index and the S&P/TSX Composite Index, while defensive sectors such as consumer staples, utilities, real estate and gold producers within materials outperformed the benchmark.

For the most part, we have yet to see a material change in operating environments or forward outlooks for most cyclical equities. Nonetheless, market participants were clearly responding to the uncertainty around tariffs — both their intensity and duration — during the quarter and the corresponding uncertainty around the broader economy.

Offsetting the cyclical weakness in the small cap market during the first quarter was a sharp move higher in gold prices. Gold closed the quarter at an all-time high of U.S. $3,123/oz — up 19% for the quarter and 39% year over year. Central bank buying and the perceived safety has pushed gold steadily higher. The heavily gold-weighted small cap materials sector followed suit, gaining 19% overall during the first quarter, with the gold sub-industry advancing a substantial 38%.

The net result was a flat, but volatile, quarter for the S&P/TSX Small Cap Total Return Index. The index was up 0.9% during the quarter versus the 1.5% return for the large cap S&P/TSX Composite Total Return Index and a 9.6% decline in CAD for the Russell 2000 — an index of U.S. small cap equities. The heavy weighting toward gold equities drove the relative outperformance of the Canadian small cap index. Ex-materials, the S&P/TSX Small Cap TRI was down 5% for the quarter.

Despite the volatile geopolitical backdrop, most macro variables were benign to start the year. The Canadian 10-year yield dropped by just 26 bps to 2.97%, and the spread to the U.S. 10-year yield tightened by 10 bps as the U.S. 10-year declined by 36 bps to 4.21%. The Canadian dollar was effectively unchanged around $1.44 CDN/USD. The West Texas Intermediate crude price was also flat, ending the quarter at approximately U.S. $71.50/bbl.

Exhibit 1: Sector Performance Varied Widely

The ClearBridge Canadian Small Cap Strategy underperformed its benchmark by ~500 bps for the quarter, with our underweight allocation to materials, particularly gold, being the primary source of the underperformance. The portfolio was also negatively impacted by underperformance in financials, energy and an overweight allocation to industrials, while relative performance was supported by IT and utilities.

Portfolio Positioning

Volatility and M&A activity allowed us to be active during the quarter. We eliminated two equities following takeout offers, added two new names to the portfolio, and were vigorous in adding to positions where we believe the market is being overly punitive.

We eliminated Canadian Western Bank following its completed combination with National Bank Financial and Converge Technology Solutions (OTCQX:CTSDF) following its announced take private transaction.

We added North American Construction Group (NOA) and Osisko Gold Royalties (OR) to the portfolio. NOA is a mining services and civil construction company that provides mine services to the Canadian oil sands (25% of revenue), Australian coal and copper mines (55%) and various civil projects, with a long history of steady, profitable growth. We believe the equity is trading at an attractive discount to intrinsic value based on unfounded concerns around contract structure in the oil sands and idle Canadian assets, which we believe can be profitably redeployed to their newly acquired Australian business.

In the case of Osisko Royalties, we have followed the name for a long time and now view the governance issues that had previously kept us on the sidelines to be behind the company. In addition, the valuation was attractive as it had materially lagged the small/mid cap gold royalties, creating a compelling entry point.

We were energetic in adding to industrials, materials, financials and energy names during the quarter at attractive discounts to intrinsic value as the market shifted away from more cyclical areas. We added to ATS Corp (ATS), Colliers International (CIGI), Propel Holdings (OTCPK:PRLPF), Calian Group (OTCPK:CLNFF), Richelieu Hardware (OTCPK:RHUHF), Methanex (MEOH) and Adentra (OTCPK:HDIUF). We view these as a collection of high-quality businesses that have been negatively affected by the broader market selloff.

We reduced our position in Trican Well Service (OTCPK:TOLWF) early in the quarter on strength and ATCO (OTCPK:ACLLF) following a strong run in the equity.

Outlook

Our investment approach is a bottom-up strategy that prioritizes identifying and capitalizing on market inefficiencies, using our proprietary research, and the time arbitrage that comes with our long-term investment horizon. This approach is supported by our patient culture, enabling us to make well-informed decisions when there are discrepancies between expectations and underlying fundamentals.

We remain steadfast in our commitment to our investment style, diligently seeking out businesses that exhibit wise capital allocation practices, maintain structural competitive advantages and can generate high-quality growth — all under appropriate capital structures.

The Trump administration’s “Liberation Day” tariffs shortly after the quarter end confirmed many of the worst fears of the market. While Canada seems to have been spared the most draconian of the U.S. tariff plans, the size and unknown duration of its tariff agenda has justifiably brought into question the outlook for inflation, employment and GDP growth globally.

Canadian market participants will have a lot to consider over the coming months: the implications of the tariffs as they stand today, a broadly more protectionist southern neighbor and a new government in Ottawa. Regardless how events unfold we believe that high-quality growth companies — with appropriate capital allocation policies, capital structures and durable competitive advantages that produce above-average returns — should be well prepared to weather the uncertainty. Moreover, we believe that these companies should be well-positioned to be proactive in improving their competitive positions in volatile markets.

Portfolio Highlights

The ClearBridge Canadian Small Cap Strategy underperformed its benchmark in the quarter. On an absolute basis, the Strategy generated gains in three of the nine sectors in which it was invested (11 total). The primary detractors from absolute returns were the cyclical financials, industrials and energy sectors while the utilities and IT sectors were the main positive contributors to absolute performance.

Relative to the benchmark, sector allocation and security selection were both negative. The Strategy was negatively impacted by an underweight position to materials and an overweight to IT, industrials, and financials. Selection was most negative in materials, utilities and financials. On the positive side, selection in the IT and industrials sectors as well as our single health care holding, DRI Healthcare Trust (OTC:DHTRF), contributed to relative results.

Among individual securities, the leading absolute contributors were Converge Technology Solutions in IT, Sandstorm Gold (SAND) in materials, AltaGas (OTCPK:ATGFF) in utilities, Empire in consumer staples and Interrent REIT (OTC:IIPZF) in real estate. The largest drags to absolute performance were Propel Holdings (OTCPK:PRLPF) in financials, Enerflex (EFXT) in energy, Methanex in materials, Adentra in industrials and Jamieson Wellness (OTCPK:JWLLF) in consumer staples.

Garey J. Aitken, CFA, Head of Canadian Equities

Michael Richmond, CFA, Portfolio Manager

data.title: GBIL: A Recession-Resilient ETF
data.description:
Thesis

One of the most surprising aspects of the recent market turmoil is the volatility associated with many short-term funds, instruments which were supposed to be equivalent to cash parking vehicles. Investors have found out the hard way that the only safe harbor during a recession or market sell-off is represented by T-Bills.

In today's article, we are going to analyze the Goldman Sachs Access Treasury 0-1 Year ETF (NYSEARCA:GBIL), a fund which we last covered almost three years ago. We are going to show investors why the name has a robust build for today's challenging markets, and why its duration profile is favorable.

When the Storm Comes, Not All Cash Parking Funds Move Equally

Let us have a look at a cohort of short-term funds, and see how they behaved during the recent market sell-off:

We are comparing pure T-Bill funds such as GBIL, iShares Treasury Floating Rate Bond ETF (TFLO) and SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (BIL) to AAA CLO names such as the Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA) and short-term bond funds such as the PIMCO Enhanced Short Maturity Active ETF (MINT) and the Janus Henderson Short Duration Income ETF (VNLA). Lastly, we have the Neos Enhanced Income 1-3 Month ETF (CSHI) which layers put spreads in order to enhance the dividend yield.

We have covered some of these names in the past, and you can find our research here:

  • TFLO: In An Uncertain World, Floating Rate Treasuries Deliver
  • BIL: Ideal Fund For Today's Environment, Over 5% Yield
  • JAAA: 'Higher For Longer' Rates Favor This CLO Fund

As we can observe from the above graph, JAAA and CSHI are by far the most volatile names, with drawdowns of -1.5%. The best performer is GBIL, followed by BIL, both treasury only funds. The rest of the cohort has modestly negative returns.

As an investor, you want positive returns during market turmoil. Cash parking vehicles are supposed to be safe heavens, and even if the drawdown is very small, it is unwarranted. The recent market action has shown us that pure treasury funds (we are using this term to include T-Bills) are the best choice during such volatile times.

The reason is simple: duration and no credit risk. JAAA has credit risk (albeit small), CSHI contains equity risk (again, a small one), VNLA and MINT also run credit risk. When the worst storm comes, credit risk will manifest itself even in the safest short-term bond funds, but not for treasuries.

Duration is Your Friend in Today's Environment

In today's environment when Fed cuts are expected, you want to have a fund with a longer duration. GBIL fits that bill with a 0.34 years option adjusted duration, and a 0.35 years weighted average maturity. The fund comes with a 0.12% net expense ratio and the Goldman expertise.

The fund contains only 39 holdings, which are all T-Bills. This translates into no credit risk for the fund, as we have seen during the recent market turmoil. The current 30-day SEC yield is 4.15%, and the fund maintains a constant NAV:

We like the fact that the fund has a NAV of 100 because it is optically very easy to identify where one's investment stands. GBIL does exactly what it is supposed to do, namely maintain a constant NAV while delivering a money market yield. Even when the equity markets are down -10% or -20%, the fund maintains its value, which is exactly how a cash parking vehicle is supposed to behave.

Fund Analytics

  • AUM: $6.3 billion
  • Expense Ratio: 0.12%
  • Year Range NAV: 99.66–100.30
  • St Dev: 0.5%
  • Composition: T-Bills

Yield Enhancement and Its Drawbacks

While GBIL is robust and reliable, it functions as a harvester of short term T-Bill yields, thus might be considered 'boring'. In the past few years we have seen the advent of 'yield enhancement' funds which use options to provide for higher dividend yields. These instruments are exciting in normalized environments since they do offer increased dividends, but are a bit of a headache during sell-offs. As we saw from the above chart, CSHI saw a very significant drawdown, and is still negative for the month.

Similarly, funds like JAAA which pay higher dividends because of the illiquid nature of their holdings, also saw significant drawdowns, making them less palatable for some investors. The take-away here is that there is no 'extra' yield without some sort of drawback. The drawback is represented by a higher level of volatility, and the possibility of having a prolonged period to get back to NAV.

Conclusion

GBIL is a fixed income exchange-traded fund. The name invests in short-dated T-bills, with a portfolio duration of 0.34 years. The ETF lacks any credit risk via its composition, and has done tremendously well in the past month when many peers have had drawdowns. An investor needs to keep in mind that any yield enhancement vehicle comes with additional risks, be it liquidity or equity risk. We have seen such vehicles experience drawdowns during the recent market moves, but not for GBIL. We find this name suitable for a recession and are a 'Strong Buy' in today's macro environment.

data.title: SMDV: Suboptimal Dividend Strategy For Tumultuous Times
data.description:
As the tariff saga continues, the markets are going through the wringer. However, not all equities are suffering equally. Some have what I would call an immunity, as while they have not been climbing significantly higher, they have not been cratering like their counterparts. A good example is the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLU). It is up marginally YTD, while the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) is deeply in the red.

There is another corner of the market that has been doing slightly better than the S&P 500 ETFs.

Dividend growth strategies, including the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF Shares (VIG), the iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO), and the ProShares S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats ETF (NOBL), though also impacted by the broad-market sell-off, have outperformed IVV this year.

It is understandable. In fact, dividend growth investing looks like a place where there is seemingly more certainty than everywhere else. The rationale here is that companies that demonstrated cash flow durability that bolstered their DPS growth over time are a nice choice for stormy weather with a prospect of a recession. However, while there is some merit in this hypothesis, not all DGI strategies are created equal, and some are clearly worth avoiding.

Today's note is supposed to offer a due update on the ProShares Russell 2000 Dividend Growers ETF (BATS:SMDV), a passively managed exchange-traded fund with a focus on SMID stocks with promising dividend growth stories.

SMDV might look like a decent choice for value-focused investors. However, I would argue that this ETF mostly underdelivers on a few fronts, so it is anything but a Buy. What are the issues?

My first article on SMDV was published in March 2022, and the previous one was presented in August 2024. Since the first analysis, I have never been especially optimistic about this vehicle, as its too small exposure to the quality factor has stimulated my skepticism.

In August, I refused to upgrade the rating even despite the fact it had beaten the S&P 500, as I argued that the outperformance was just temporary. And it seems I was right.

It has also trailed IVV YTD.

So below, I would like to discuss what exactly has contributed to SMDV's losses, how its factor story has changed, especially what the disappointments on the dividend factor front are, and why I believe a Hold rating should be maintained.

What is SMDV's Strategy?

As I would like to put it, the core idea of SMDV's strategy is to offer exposure to SMID names that have been demonstrating some zeal for increasing their dividends and have already shown some consistency but are yet far from achieving a status of mid-cap dividend aristocrats with 15 consecutive years of dividend increases.

More specifically, as we know from SMDV's website, it has been tracking the Russell 2000® Dividend Growth Index since its inception in February 2015. From the summary prospectus, we know that:

  1. Its constituents are taken from the Russell 2000 Index.
  2. Companies that "have consistently increased dividends each year for at least the last 10 years" can qualify.
  3. The goal is to select "at least 40 companies."
  4. The equal weighting schema is applied, with a 30% single sector cap.

SMDV Performance Discussion

As explained on the page 3 of the summary prospectus,

The Index’s composition is reevaluated annually in June and the weightings are reset to equal weight each quarter in March, June, September, and December.

In fact, just two stocks have been removed since my August note was published, namely Atrion Corporation (traded with a ticker ATRI), which was acquired by Nordson (NDSN), and Premier Financial Corp. (traded with a ticker PFC), which was acquired by WesBanco (WSBC).

Stock Weight as of August 16
ATRI 0.92%
PFC 1.14%

Data from the ETF

So as deletions were minor and there were no additions, using the share prices as of August 16, 2024, and April 12, 2025, we can make an in-depth assessment of what stocks and sectors were the main culprits for SMDV's losses since my previous article was published. I believe the best way to present the key findings is in the table like the one below:

Sector Number of stocks Stocks with negative price returns Weight (as of April 11) Median price return The worst performer
Communication 2 2 1.9% -13.3% Cogent Communications Holdings, Inc. (CCOI), -29.6%
Consumer Discretionary 1 1 0.9% -37.0% Johnson Outdoors (JOUT), -37%
Consumer Staples 7 6 7.4% -12.6% The Andersons, Inc. (ANDE), -24.6%
Financials 30 28 29.1% -11.3% Atlantic Union Bankshares Corporation (AUB), -36.3%
Health Care 2 2 2.3% -7.2% The Ensign Group, Inc. (ENSG), -9.4%
Industrials 25 17 25.3% -11.2% Hyster-Yale (HY), -37%
Information Technology 2 2 1.8% -15.5% Power Integrations (POWI), -31.7%
Materials 6 6 5.7% -13.1% Stepan Company (SCL), -36.3%
Real Estate 5 4 5.1% -11.4% NexPoint Residential Trust (NXRT), -22.8%
Utilities 18 7 20.3% -3.4% Otter Tail Corporation (OTTR), -11.5%

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETF

So what we can deduce from the table above is that, if SMDV's exposure to utilities, the (almost) recession-immune sector, so it tends to suffer less during market declines, was not that sizable, its returns would have been even worse amid the recent sell-off.

Looking at the longer-term returns, the picture is also rather grim, though not without bright spots.

We see that over the March 2015-March 2025 period, SMDV delivered significantly weaker annualized returns than the WisdomTree U.S. MidCap Dividend ETF (DON) and the ProShares S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrats ETF (REGL), let alone IVV. The good news is that it beat the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) and captured just 82.07% of IVV's downside, a nice result compared to those of the selected peers, plus its decline in its worst year (2022, during the previous bear market) was only 5.88%.

SMDV Portfolio Analysis

At first glance, little has changed in the SMDV portfolio since my August 2024 coverage. However, despite the adjustments being minor, a few changes in the factor story can be identified upon a more attentive review.

Sector mix

There have been some changes in sector proportions driven by the performance of the holdings.

More specifically, we see that the weight of financials, SMDV's top allocation both in August 2024 and April 2025, declined, while industrials and utilities gained. This is unsurprising as the latter two have been doing much better recently.

Factor mix: stronger growth, with nuances

A few nuances in the factor trifecta (value, growth, and quality) can be identified as well.

For instance, we see that SMDV's weighted-average market cap touched the lowest level since March 2024. Expectedly, the adjusted earnings yield has improved while Price/Sales and Enterprise Value/EBITDA have declined, plus the share of stocks with a B- Quant Valuation rating or higher has increased.

What is certainly surprising and seemingly complicated to explain is that SMDV's WA forward EPS growth rate has more than doubled. This looks especially puzzling when juxtaposed to the decline in the forward EBITDA growth rate and just a slight increase in the forward revenue growth rate. I have delved a bit deeper into this discrepancy and found out that for about 55.6% of the holdings, EPS growth forecasts have been revised higher. There is a sizable group of utilities among them, 11 names with 12.4% weight. However, financials are primarily responsible for this improvement (24 stocks, 23.3% weight).

As to quality, I have been criticizing it since the first note, and there is no reason why I should become more positive on it today. In fact, SMDV's exposure to stocks with a B- Quant Profitability rating or higher of about 35% is critically low.

Dividends: growth rates disappoint

I believe the dividend factor should be addressed separately.

First, we see that SMDV's weighted-average dividend yield has slightly improved, and this is driven chiefly by one factor - its dismal performance.

Second, what I believe is the main disappointment is that the WA dividend growth rates have slipped. There were a few reasons for that. More specifically, both Atrion and Premier Financial were significant contributors to the 3-year and 5-year dividend CAGRs, so their removal had adversely impacted the weighted-average rates.

However, the bigger problem here is that these CAGRs have declined for 74% of the current holdings. For context, a good example here is TXNM Energy, Inc. (TXNM), which has a 1.2% weight in the SMDV portfolio, and HNI Corporation (HNI), with a 1.06% weight.

Stock 3Y Dividend CAGR (as of April 2025) 3Y Dividend CAGR (as of August 2024)
TXNM 5.69% 14.32%
HNI 2.11% 9.69%

Data from Seeking Alpha

I believe this could be the consequence of the overall soft quality of this portfolio. That is to say, low quality (when it comes to margins, capital efficiency, stability of cash flows, etc.) means that at some point, a company will face a decision whether it has to scale back capex and opex (sometimes a dangerous move as it can jeopardize competitive advantages and the long-term growth story overall) or reduce the rate of its DPS increases in order not to face a situation when dividend growth stops completely. That is why I believe that for the DGI portfolios, quality should be the top priority.

Final Thoughts

I am of the opinion that it is worth ignoring SMDV. At first blush, it has a solid proposition with a focus on SMID names with no less than a decade of unhindered dividend growth. However, its portfolio is too heavy in low-quality stocks, plus its returns are anything but robust.

So while I have been thinking about a Sell rating, assuming tremendous volatility and record uncertainty, I decided that constructing a bearish thesis would be a bold move. And owing to the balance of positives (like its downside capture ratio of around 82%) and negatives, I decided to proceed with a Hold rating.

data.title: Western Alliance Bank Shines On Margins, While Loan Chargeoffs May Cloud The Sky
data.description:
Intro: A Regional Bank In The American West, On A Roller-Coaster Ride Lately

With recent market volatility, today's article returns to a sector I covered often on this platform and that is regional banks, with the goal of finding dip-buying and undervalued opportunities on otherwise fundamentally solid businesses.

One of those banks, Phoenix-based Western Alliance Bancorp (NYSE:WAL), the parent of Western Alliance Bank, has seen its share price dive 13% over the last month.

Because of such a short-term roller coaster in markets, today's thesis is geared to investors focused on a longer-term horizon rather than day trading, and looking beyond the recent volatile day-to-day swings in bank stocks.

From its Seeking Alpha profile, we know this firm "provides various banking products and related services primarily in Arizona, California, and Nevada. It operates through Commercial and Consumer Related segments."

With its most recent dividend yield hovering above 2%, this stock is also one of my dividend ideas for the week, and my rating for this stock will be based on 9 factors I consider holistically to determine an average rating score, which includes a section on the effects of public policy, since we have seen how policy decisions can impact markets and stocks.

Thesis: An Undervalued Regional Bank With Expected Future Price Growth

My thesis today will present why I think this stock is a buying opportunity, and will consider 9 different factors, assigning a rating score to each.. with an average score determining whether it is a buy, sell, or hold.

In summary, as shown in the table below, I gave this stock an average rating score of "7" which justifies a buy rating.

Some key positive factors driving upside include: undervaluation and expected future price growth, the recent upgrade from Fitch, a dividend yield beating its sector, and a proven profit margin strength.

At the same time, some negatives to consider are that this stock did not have a great 10-year total return compared to the S&P500 index, it can be exposed to the rising trend of nonperforming loans and charge-offs, as well as interest rate risk.

The following article goes into each topic with a combination of third-party evidence, some historic data, and some future-minded guidance /outlooks too considering that FY25 Q1 results are due soon, so this article aims at previewing the firm to get ahead of the next earnings call.

Company's Positive Outlook For 2025 Could Depend On More Loan Growth Like In 2024

When it comes to the business of regional banks like this one, I think what could drive future revenue growth is recent growth in loans as that drives future interest income, but also business acquisitions of other banks and its customer base along with new branch openings. Consider, for example, that loans are usually something a customer might pay years of interest on.

Notable from this firm's FY24 Q4 presentation from late January, Q4 saw YoY loan growth of 6.7%, which I believe is a solid upside factor as it drives interest income:

From my research, it seems this bank essentially makes most of its revenue from interest, both on loans and on its securities portfolio, and makes some revenue from fees, but does not really have any major fee-driven business like asset management or wealth advisory, as big banks like Morgan Stanley (MS) may have.

Because of this, future revenue can be impacted a lot more heavily by interest rate yields, and the firm did mention the effect of lower yields in their Q4 presentation.

For example, the following chart shows a slight YoY drop in yield on their securities portfolio, however it was nothing too drastic:

Hence, a macro factor that could impact this bank looking ahead is loan demand.

The company did not mention any business acquisitions recently, or new branch growth, that I could find, however the firm did add "an aerospace and defense banker team, and a food and agriculture banker team, both based in California," according to a fall 2024 article in industry site Banking Dive, as the bank attempts greater penetration of those business segments, which could drive future growth I think, setting this regional bank up as a specialist in specific business banking segments.

As the above factors could be positive drivers, in terms of revenue growth the company's own outlook for FY25 called for +6-8% growth in both interest and non-interest income, which I think could attract bullish investors putting more weight on growth, thereby adding support to the share price.

Earnings Growth Can Be Driven By Continued Solid Profit Margin Within Sector

When getting a sense for this bank's earnings growth potential, a metric I like to consider is the net income margin, or profit margin, to get an idea for the firm's efficiency at turning a profit vs similar peers in the same sector.

From Seeking Alpha data, we can see that the firm achieved a margin of 26%, about 16% better than its sector average.

To get more granular, I picked 4 regional banks to compare to, in different regions of the US (Texas, Southeast, Mid-Atlantic, Northeast) and they are: Cullen-Frost (CFR), Farmers National (FMNB), Regions (RF), and Valley National (VLY). We can see from the table below that Western Alliance Bank has held up well among this peer group of regionals, and whose margin is only around 5% lower than the group average.

Another metric to point out is from the firm's Q4 presentation, and that is the efficiency ratio, since non-interest expenses like operating costs are a major component that can impact the bottom line and future earnings.

Although the company beat its sector in profit margin, a challenge I think it will face is its efficiency ratio has seen a YoY decline as of Q4, as non-interest expenses creeped higher:

So, what I think investors should look for in the upcoming Q1 earnings results is whether this negative trend in the efficiency ratio has continued, which could indicate the company will need to take more steps to control costs.

A strong margin can contribute to solid net income results and EPS, and notable to mention is that this firm's EPS growth (GAAP) both on a YoY and fwd basis has exceeded its sector average. For example, its fwd EPS growth is +17% while the sector average is just under 10%.

This is relevant because the next EPS will be a key headline metric that both investors and analysts will react to, and I am weighing more towards it being a positive reaction. Consider that it was reported on Apr. 10th by Yahoo! Finance and Zack's Market Research that "the market expects Western Alliance to deliver a year-over-year increase in earnings on higher revenues when it reports results for the quarter ended March 2025."

Public Policy Is Driving Continually Changing Sentiment, But Not Indication To Panic

In light of recent market events, I added a new section going forward and here I will briefly talk about the effects of public policy on this firm and sector.

For instance, a Reuters article on Apr. 3rd pointed out that if tariff policy leads to a recessionary environment, an effect could be that "it will impact loan growth and credit quality," however also pointing out that "the U.S. banking sector has broadly been performing well with robust earnings and non-performing loans under check, prompting some analysts to predict the sector may yet weather the challenges without a significant hit to earnings."

So, I don't believe headline-inducing tariff policy by itself will necessarily make or break this sector, since tariffs were not invented yesterday, and it is more about whether such tariffs would trigger a widespread US recession and drop in loan demand, but also ability for businesses to pay their debts if a recession leads to lower incomes.

As for interest rates, that is more in the realm of the Federal Reserve which is not a federal cabinet agency but still deserves mention within public policy.

I would note that Fed rate tracker CME Fedwatch (as of Sunday evening data) now indicates an 60% probability the Fed keeps its target interest rate the same after its May 7th meeting, while last week it had been closer to 83%, so it appears the probability of a rate cut has increased. For June's Fed meeting, it predicts a nearly 50% chance of a rate cut.

My opinion is that the rate cut may be expected as a way to stave off a recession. If this translates into lower interest rates across the board, I expect a boost in loan demand as debt gets cheaper.

Cheaper debt may not necessarily be a huge demand driver for bank loans if there is a large enough recession, though, as businesses and consumers may both cut back on borrowing.

A key headline that impacted markets was the recession outlook from a major bank, as reported in Investopedia on Apr. 7th, and other media too, when Goldman Sachs had cut their GDP growth forecast for 2025, citing tariff risk.

Interestingly, just days after that and by the middle of last week they reversed course. As reported by the Wall St Journal on Apr. 9th:

We are reverting to our previous non-recession baseline forecast with GDP growth of 0.5% and a 45% probability of recession.

Complementary to this new update, according to a news article by Seeking Alpha last week, NYC-based Fundstrat Advisors said the following:

Recession risks remain overblown due to significant offsets that cushion the tariff blow.. From a tactical perspective, markets are deeply oversold and ripe for a reversal.

It is also worth mentioning that another major financial firm, Wells Fargo, said last Tuesday that " they're seeing a handful of promising signs that the US economy may be able to avoid a downturn this year," according to Business Insider, as two positive factors were rising incomes and household wealth.

So, I don't think there is an overly strong case in support of a severe recession happening, at least not one that would dampen loan demand, particularly if Fed rate cuts boosts demand for cheap debt.

Balance Sheet Risk Improved With Fitch Upgrade, Though Risks Remain

When it comes to balance sheet risk, for this type of business what comes to mind is how much potential loan loss its loan book is exposed to, what kind of real estate exposure it has, and also its corporate debt and bond holdings.

One bright spot to mention is that this bank has lowered its debt/equity over the last 5 years down to 0.96, indicating lower corporate debt in relation to equity, which I think is a positive factor.

The trend I am more cautious about is the increasing trend we see in non-performing loans, and the Q4 presentation clearly shows a YoY uptick (though a small one) in that trend, although NPLs still remain less than 0.70% of loans:

Paired with that, the same results (figures in millions$$) point to an upward trend of loan charge-offs comparing on a YoY basis, which could indicate clients having increasing difficulty paying back debts.

Consider a March article by Marketplace.org, which pointed to a larger issue and it is primarily consumer credit card debt:

In 2024, Americans defaulted on $59 billion in credit card debt — a 34% jump from 2023.. inflation is a huge factor.

This bank also made an effort to mention its exposure to commercial real estate, which I think is a good thing as investor will want to know about that.

From its Q4 presentation, it appears that 18% of its total loan book is tied to commercial real estate, however that CRE exposure is diversified across multiple sub-segments, with 24% exposed to office property. So, in the larger picture, exposure to office real estate in relation to the overall size of the loan portfolio is not relatively huge.

I should also mention that major agency Fitch reported in February 2025 that it upgraded this bank to a "BBB" which is an investment-grade rating, saying one key factor behind the upgrade was "increased capital levels and improved ratios, alongside a reduction in the company's loan-to-deposit (LTD) ratio."

Finally, its fixed-income securities portfolio is certainly subject to interest rate risk, and we already mentioned the likelihood of Fed rate cuts soon, but recently we also saw Treasury yields jump after a bond selloff, so more bond selloffs could impact asset values for firms with a large bond portfolio but also boost yields which could lead to more interest income from higher-yielding bonds.

A Lagging Factor is The 10 Year Return vs S&P500 Index

Particularly since short-term returns on stocks, as we have seen over the last few weeks, can swing wildly, in this section I wanted to get a closer look at what kind of total return (which includes dividend income as well as price return) this stock has achieved over the last 10 year period, compared to a common index being tracked like the S&P500 index.

Using momentum data from Seeking Alpha, I created the following table that compares WAL vs the index, showing its total return has underperformed the index by nearly 30%.

While this may reflect historical performance and not future, I am including it to show whether this stock has proven itself over a longer term in either being close to or beating a major index, which it has not really done well so far.

Many stocks are impacted by recent price dives, but my question is how well some of them recover and achieve a total return for its investors in the longer run, and I think a good benchmark is being +/- 10% vs the S&P 500, or far outperforming it.

Hence, this stock got a lower score in this category for this reason, pulling down its overall average rating score a bit.

Dividend Yield Lags Behind Several Regional Banks

When it comes to another dividend component, yield, I would not put this stock in a high-yield category, with a yield of just 2.4% as of this article writing, while the sector average is closer to 3.5%.

Among the regional banking comparison group I created earlier, here is a comparison of their dividend yields, placing this bank last among the group, so if I was a new buyer and building a high-yield portfolio was my primary focus it seems a bank like New-Jersey based Valley National with its +5% yield is more suitable for that type of goal:

This above-average yield gave this stock a little boost in its average rating score, and could particularly be of interest to yield-driven investors.

A Proven Return on Equity Among Peers

Another factor I have added, starting with this article, is whether a firm has a proven model to achieve strong return on equity, either meeting or exceeding its sector and or peer group, as a benchmark to compare against.

In particular this is an interesting data point considering the vast amount of assets this bank holds.

We can see that this bank's return on common equity was 12.71%, well above the sector average of 10.4%, which is already impressive.

Also, when I compared against the peer group mentioned earlier, this bank stands up strong being 2nd place within this group pf 5 regionals:

Although this data may be based on historic results and not future projections, I think it lays a strong foundation for this firm to present an attractive case for investors who put more emphasis on having a proven model for achieving a strong return on shareholder equity.

Share Price & Valuation Took A Major Dip Below The Average, Creating Potential Opportunity

In this section, we will take a look at both the share price and a few valuation metrics.

Putting aside the short-term volatility of the last week, I created this chart to show this stock's price movement over 5 years vs its 200-day SMA and 5 year high/low:

Although the stock got a boost, along with many others, on Wednesday after a pause on tariffs for many countries was announced, the stock is still over 20% below its 200-day SMA, going into Monday. It also shows that this stock can be impacted greatly in the short-term by sudden public policy announcements.

Further, Seeking Alpha's valuation data shows metrics like the fwd P/E (GAAP) down to 7.27, well below the sector average, while the trailing P/B is around 1.07 and almost in line with the sector average.

This appears like value-driven investors on a shopping spree this week may take a closer look at this company, considering such low valuations and price dip.

My own price target for this stock is $62.96 for Dec. 2025, $75.46 for Dec. 2026, and $82.22 for Dec. 2027, so based on my scenario a longer-term investor holding this stock at least a few years could see a +30% gain by Dec. 2027 if this projection is realized:

The guidance is based on the fwd P/E multiple right now multiplied by the analyst consensus estimate on EPS for those periods. This is also mentioned as a common price target methodology in sites like Public.com, where the Price Target = Projected EPS × Expected P/E Ratio.

From my guidance above, a shorter-term investor might not benefit as much as a longer-term one.

Additional Sector Risks: Market Competition And Cyber Incidents

I added this section to include any sector-driven risk factors that have not already been mentioned, and give those factors either an upside or downside score, depending on what I think its impact would be to this bank.

One risk factor to consider is stiff market competition within banking, as with any sector, and what kind of market leadership position this firm has as a "brand".

From Federal Reserve data, we can see that this bank is merely ranked #33 among US commercial banks, placing it well behind large names who may also be doing business in those western regions of the US this bank calls home.

Other data, from the Phoenix Business Journal , says although Western Alliance has the largest deposit base locally in Phoenix, it is being chased right behind by JP Morgan Chase (JPM), who is also the largest bank in the US, and also is competing closely for deposits in the entire state.

So, investors should consider that even though this may be an important regional player and a major bank in Phoenix and Arizona, the large national banking names are increasingly at its heels and trying to capture that regional market share, but also have the vast scale and resources to do so.

Cyber risks can be headline-inducing for this sector, and can hurt brand reputation, as we have seen with the March 21st article in Security Magazine who reported that 22,000 customers were impacted by a data breach at Western Alliance Bank. In fact, it would be almost irresponsible for me as an analyst not to have mentioned this incident while covering this company.

The "flaw was found in a third-party software, and it was used to hack a select few Western Alliance systems to exfiltrate files," according to the article.

This type of incident, I think, underscores how vulnerable banks are, much like insurance firms, as they hold vast amounts of sensitive client data which can be stolen.

For the above two reasons, stiff market competition and potential for large-scale cyber attacks that impact business and brand reputation, I gave this firm a slightly lower score in the category of "sector risk factors," which lowers it overall from potentially being a strong buy.

Conclusion: Buy The Undervaluation

In summary, my research has determined this stock is a buy.

Here is the rating table I used, with my weighted scores, and the key used:

To summarize today's thesis, this stock just passed into the buy category from potentially being only a hold, as several categories weighed more towards the buy side this time, and I showed why in the previous sections.

I gave the largest buy-side weight to the current valuation and 3-year price targets, which I determined to be favorable for longer-term investors. In addition, the public policy risks of recesssion-driving tariffs and interest rate decisions by the Fed did not present enough downside this time around, from evidence I found.

data.title: SoFi Technologies' Growth Engine Just Shifted Gears
data.description:
SoFi Technologies (NASDAQ:SOFI) is no longer the niche digital lender it was in the past. Over the last few years, it has grown increasingly to be a full-stack, vertically integrated financial platform offering lending, banking, investments, credit cards, and infrastructure technology under one digital umbrella.

SoFi is evolving from a lending-first disruptor into a diversified fintech platform with powerful earnings potential. In 2024, fee-based segments made up nearly half of revenue, signaling a major pivot toward capital-light, high-ROE growth. With expanding product monetization and strategic execution, SoFi is building a durable engine for long-term compounding.

From Lending Giant to Fintech Flywheel

Its 2024 performance was one of the pinnacle moments in this journey. It recorded historic revenue, reported five consecutive GAAP profitable quarters, and brought fee-based revenue almost to half of the total adjusted net revenue. SoFi's adjusted net revenue in 2024 was $2.61 billion, 26% greater than last year. More indicative of the strength at hand, though, is the acceleration in non-lending revenue.

Its Technology Platform and Financial Services businesses recorded $1.2 billion in revenue, or 54% year-over-year, and now represent 47% of total revenue, versus 38% in 2023. It's diversification that graphically describes the intentional execution of a strategy to reduce the company's reliance on net interest income and establish a higher-ROE, capital-light business.

Loans, SoFi's largest category, accounted for $1.45 billion of adjusted revenue, driven by $23.2 billion in mortgage, student, and personal loan originations. Its in-house banking subsidiary, fueled by $25 billion of member deposits, has driven funding cost reductions and balance sheet management that other fintech can't.

Lending as the Foundation: Scaling Through Optionality and Efficiency

In Lending, profitability and growth are driven by personal loans. These loans are resilient in high interest environments, while both home lending and student loan refinancing are interest rate-sensitive and cyclic. SoFi's balance sheet approach is rooted in optionality: it keeps a proportion of the loans to earn interest income, sells a proportion through whole-loan channels, and increasingly originates directly to institutions through LPB.

SoFi sold $5.8 billion of whole loans in 2024, securitized $1.2 billion, and sourced $2.1 billion through LPB to partners such as Fortress. Its hybrid model maximizes capital productivity without compromising member acquisition while de-risking the balance sheet. Significantly, the LPB model enables SoFi to keep servicing rights and member relationships and presents downstream cross-sell opportunities even if loans are off its books.

The Financial Services division, which features SoFi Invest, SoFi Money, credit cards, and newer offerings such as Relay and SoFi at Work, achieved profitability in its inaugural year. It is a significant achievement in light of its capital-light status. Most notably, new credit card launches and the robo-advisor platform drove greater engagement and increased monetization potential. Brokerage, interchange, and advisory referral fees drove most of the revenue in this division. As these products mature and value per user for life rises,

Financial Services can potentially equal Lending in terms of profit contribution. Its profitability in early stages remains delicate, however, and consistent gains will rely on minimizing the cost of customer acquisition and rising average revenue per user through high-frequency use.

Galileo and Technisys are the core of SoFi's vision to serve as an infrastructure platform for the fintech and banking world. In 2024, Galileo processed over one billion transactions and introduced its U.S. core banking product. Those victories, together with the Direct Express deal, set the Technology Platform on course for recurring revenue in the longer term. Contribution margins are, however, modest, given the pressure on prices and cost of integration. Future potential depends on layering in additional products, fraud protection, compliance APIs, and identity solutions that yield better margins. Expansion internationally, especially in Latin America, may also spur growth if managed correctly, though there is growing competition in banking-as-a-service.

SoFi’s Next Test: Can It Outrun Its Own Success?

Ahead of SoFi's next earnings, the market is anticipating a slowdown, or at least on paper. Following a record Q4 2024 in which the company beat per-share earnings expectations, the full-year 2025 consensus earnings now stand at $0.26 per share, implying a 33% drop YoY. However, the earnings decline is somewhat misleading, as last year’s results were boosted by a one-time deferred tax benefit.

The quarterly pace is still intact. SoFi beat Q1, Q2, and Q3 2024 estimates by a penny each quarter, leading to the enormous Q4 beat. SoFi is expected to see steady EPS growth in Q1, Q2, and Q3 of FY2025, followed by a significant decline in Q4 due to the comp last year. Revenue estimates remain at $3.17 billion in 2025.

Investors also need to listen intently to management discussions of cross-sell traction, Lending margin strength, and additional monetization of the LPB and Galileo platforms. If SoFi can keep growing fee-based revenue, drive CAC down, and show better unit economics, it may well beat as well as meet these lofty expectations.

If, however, margin squeeze or integration issues arise, the stock will be vulnerable, given its premium valuation. Having set a high bar and visibility lengthening, this next quarter's report may determine if 2024 was a break-out year or only the start.

Valuation Aligned to Implementation: A Premium with Performance Requirements

SoFi's valuation today puts it in the higher-multiple group of names in the fintech and financial services group. As of early 2025, it trades for roughly 42x forward earnings on the consensus 2025 EPS estimate of $0.26. This is comparable to 21x on 2026 expectations, which would involve nearly doubling earnings to $0.50. Based on the estimates, SoFi is trading on about 3.6x forward price/sales in 2025 and 3.04x in 2026. Those figures signal that the market is imbuing high confidence in the company's capacity to maintain its growth rate, expand margins, and expand monetization throughout the ecosystem.

Relative to traditional banking or even certain digital-native peers, SoFi's valuation appears aggressive on the surface. To cite a couple of examples, Upstart (UPST) and LendingClub (LC) are valued on notably lower revenue and EBITDA multiples, driven by their exposure to cyclical lending and the absence of platform diversification. Block (XYZ), even though it's got a larger ecosystem in which to operate, is valued on a lower revenue multiple while also boasting better historic margins.

However, SoFi's premium is not simply a reflection of growth, it's a wager on strategic execution. What investors are really baking in is the continuation of SoFi's successful transition to fee-based revenue, disciplined operating leverage, and capacity to realize value on its expanding user base through a high-retention, multi-product strategy.

But this premium valuation comes with a tight margin of error. An LPB volume stumble, worse-than-anticipated adoption of financial services products, or teething issues in integrating Galileo and Technisys may trigger a derating. Investors will need to compare the potential upsides of SoFi becoming a financial super-app and enterprise platform versus the risk of macro headwinds or operational misfires that might impede its compounding flywheel.

For long-term investors, the current multiple may be fair if the company can maintain 25–30% adjusted EBITDA margins and grow EPS by a 40–50% CAGR over the next three years. However, in the near term, modest underperformance versus expectations may trigger volatility.

A Fintech Compounding Machine in the Making, But Not Without Risks

The investment thesis in SoFi is strong, particularly for investors looking for exposure to the long-term trend toward digital-first finance. Its vertical integration, well-established brand among younger customers, and diversifying model of revenue provide it with a platform that few other fintechs enjoy.

Its flywheel is gaining traction as members purchase additional products, financial service monetization increases, and the lending business becomes profitable and well-leveraged. In contrast to its neobank peers, which rely on interchange or high-yield savings tricks, SoFi enjoys a layered ecosystem, both consumer-facing and enterprise, that is starting to exhibit operating leverage.

Nevertheless, there are real risks. Regulatory attention is likely to increase, notably in terms of banking charters and LPB businesses. An extended environment of high rates may depress origination demand, notably in rate-sensitive products like mortgages. LPB scaling-up is encouraging, although it adds vulnerability to partners such as Fortress. Although there are longer-term benefits in Galileo and Technisys, execution risk exists, notably in scaling B2B infrastructure profitably.

SoFi's valuation today is predicated on faith in the narrative, and it also builds in very little margin for error. But for those willing to risk near-term volatility and watch important measurements such as contribution margin, product adoption speed, and cross-sell penetration, SoFi presents the potential to invest in one of the few fintechs that would end up being a structural compounder.

The journey from fintech disruptor to market leader is never linear. But in SoFi's situation, the pieces are falling in place, and if management keeps executing, the reward can be significant.

Bottom Line

SoFi's growth narrative is true, yet maintaining expanding margins and cross-segment execution will be the challenge test. For investors, the risk-reward is predicated upon its capability of converting innovation to consistent, diversified cash flow rather than simple user growth.

6 - Shell plc

Energy IOC/ Integrated Oil & Gas Company headquartered in London, UK. since 1907 with an “A+” S&P credit rating.

5 year Seeking Alpha Price and technical chart
