7 - Home Depot

Retail home improvement consumer cyclical headquartered in Atlanta, GA and incorporated in 1978 has an “A” S&P credit rating.

5 year Seeking Alpha Price and technical chart

The dividend raise of 5c from $2.25 to $2.30= 2.2%, giving it a 15-year record of raising it.

Yearly dividend of $9.20 gives it a yield of 2.6% at a price of $353.86 with a payout of 59.1%.

5-year DGR = 10.6%, so the recent raise was completely way low and unappreciated.

The earnings that were down in 2024 by 9%, look to rise 1% in 2025 but fall by 1% in 2026, so that is what I call flat and most likely the reason for a flat low and lousy dividend raise. Earnings estimates do look up double digits or 10% for 2027 and 2028 while the dividend looks to rise better but not into double digits.

Chowder # = 13.2 (10.6 +2.6) which is above the 12 needed to pass for a stock with a yield over 2.5%.

Rose Take and Recommendation

It is a quality safe dividend, but the low raise makes the C# metric not appropriate, and I would wait on an even lower price to make it a buy.

8 - Broadcom

Technology semiconductor company headquartered in Palo Alto, CA. has a BBB- S&P credit rating.

5 year Seeking Alpha Price and technical chart

The dividend raise of 6c from 53c to 59c= 11.3% giving it a 15-year record of raising it.

The yearly dividend of $2.36 gives it a yield of 1.3% at a price of $181.94 with a payout of 43.22%.

5-year DGR = 14.78%, so the raise was wonderful but still a bit under expectations.

Using “FG” earnings look to rise by 36% for 2025, but they are down to mid double digits of 18% for 2026 and 16% for 2027. The current P/E even with the price decline is ~32, which goes good with 36% earnings but not with lower future estimates as mentioned.

Chowder # = 16.08 (14.78 +1.3) is above the 15 needed to pass for a growth stock with a yield under 2.5%.

Rose Take and Recommendation

Quality tech stock with low dividend payout but probably getting better valued as the price drops. I consider it a hold and watch for earnings reports to show higher guidance to own more.

Summary/ Conclusion

“RIG” is constructed as an income portfolio and all the stocks have dividends, making it important to have them give raises. All of the 8 stocks that gave raises are staying in "RIG" but will constantly be under review for growing future dividends. The income is on the rise by 5.1% from 2024. The current forward yield is 6.44% with a portfolio value that has followed the recent market declines by being down 1.45% YTD but still beats SPY by 7.49% which is down 8.9% YTD.

Happy Investing to All.

data.title: Market Outlook: What Will Happen Next Week
data.description:
Introduction

Last week, the markets again were very volatile. While we saw many deep red days, we also saw the second largest intraday range ever in the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:SPY) and Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (NASDAQ:QQQ). In this post, I will give you an overview of what might happen during next week.

Data Releases

Next week, there won't be that many very important US data releases. On Wednesday, US Retail Sales will be published. While they increased by 0.2% the last time, the market is now pricing in a gigantic 1.3% increase, which would be the highest in 1 year. As a result, a regression analysis (shown in Figure 1) yields a far lower expected increase of around 0.3%. This means either that there should have happened a fundamental shift in the market or that the market is overestimating the potential growth rate of retail sales.

Another important US data release is on Building Permits and Housing Starts on Friday. Building Permits are expected to be at 1.46M, slightly up from the latest reported number of 1.459M. Additionally, housing starts are expected to be lower than the last time, at 1.41M instead of 1.501M. The regression in Figure 2 shows the data points for housing starts. While it agrees with the expectations of lower starts, it estimates them to be even lower at 1.33M. Nevertheless, this should only be seen as an estimation of the trend and not as a reliable forecasting technique.

Apart from US data, there is much to unpack. On the night from Tuesday to Wednesday, China will report its GDP growth rate. The market currently shows a consensus of 5.1%, down from 5.4%. Additionally, we will see the BOC interest rate decision on Wednesday, while the ECB interest rate decision follows on Thursday. The BOC is expected not to cut interest rates, the market prices in a 25bp rate cut from the ECB. We will end the week with Japan reporting inflation data on Friday.

In terms of earnings, we will see The Goldman Sachs Group (GS) report earnings on Monday at a consensus EPS of $12.71. On Tuesday, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Bank of America Corporation (BAC), and Citigroup Inc. (C) will follow at estimated EPS of $2.57, $0.81, and $1.84, respectively. The last big earnings release will be by Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company on Thursday. Here, the market prices in an EPS of $2.02.

Outlook

Figure 3 shows the VIX. As can be seen, the index is currently at 37.55, which still signals extreme volatility. Since volatility is a great predictor of volatility and since none of the fundamental problems driving stock market volatility have been resolved, we can expect another extremely volatile week. The focus of investors will again be tariffs, with even more potential in the US-China trade war being the main concern. Apart from that, investors will likely focus on possible trade deals that will be made or that fail. As Trump said last week, if negotiations fail, the 90-day delay on tariffs could be taken back for some countries. Even if this only happens to one country, the news could be detrimental for the market. Additionally, Trump could even want to fail negotiations to show other countries that they have to give him even more than they currently want to. Nevertheless, in the night from Friday to Saturday, an exemption of US import tariffs for phones, computers, and other electronics was released. This will likely cause the markets to rise on Monday, with QQQ benefiting the most as it is quite tech-heavy. Nevertheless, the news should not be taken as being that bullish, since Trump got nothing in return and put himself in a worse position to negotiate tariffs against China back to where they were a few weeks ago. Because of this, I remain rather bearish, although the exemption is a step in the right direction.

In bonds such as the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (NASDAQ:TLT), we will likely see another week of uncertainty. In my opinion, prices are likely to continue falling as long as there is no clear credit event happening that signals to the market that the Fed will cut. While there are hopes for a rate cut, the more likely scenario will be for the Fed to wait and see how tariffs will affect inflation. I will talk about this more in a few weeks when the Fed meeting is coming up. Concerning the potential credit event, Figure 4 shows the moving averages of the weekly bankruptcy filings. As can be seen, the 4-week moving average currently shows an upward spike that was last seen during 2008 and 2020. This makes shorting treasuries very risky at the moment.

Conclusion

Since the upcoming week will not have extremely important data releases in the US markets, the focus will likely lie on tariffs again, especially considering Trump's recent announcement of tariff exemptions for smartphones. While a rally on Monday can be expected, the short- to medium-term outlook remains rather bearish. Nevertheless, I will upgrade QQQ from a “strong sell” to only a “sell”, while SPY remains at “sell”. Concerning treasuries, I revise my recent “strong sell” rating to a “hold” rating as the tail risk of a credit event balances out the expected return of continuing to short them.

data.title: Alphabet May Become The Largest Company In The World, Here's How
data.description:
Alphabet May Become the Largest Company in the World or get mighty close, Here’s How

Tough market

This week has not been easy for the market participant, whether a trader, medium-term speculator, or buy-and-hold investor. Even the pros are having a hard time as hedge funds have reported losses on either side of the trade, long or short. This is a yo-yo market where stocks shoot up 12% one day, then down big the next, then up big again. What’s the little guy to do? Since the 3rd week of February, I have been moving away from stressing trading, where now I just don’t. When this VIX is this high, the volatility is enormous, trading is really dangerous. Once the VIX comes back down to 17-16-15, I am avoiding it. What I have been doing is concentrating on my investment account, and concentrating on the best quality large capitalization stocks as they have been coming down. For the last several years, I had relatively small positions in the Mag7 names, and looked for medium-sized stocks that were dislocated from their valuation, and traded at a discount that I could gain from. With big-cap tech selling at a discount of as much as 30%, concentrating on growing positions here made sense.

Some in the financial media said, Mag 7 stocks were approaching bubble territory at their highs. I don’t think so

Some might counter this logic by saying that the old highs were unrealistic, and should not be used as an attainable target. It has been my experience that at some point, sometimes very soon, large-cap growth, indeed all sizes growth stocks of quality, usually regain their old highs.

Furthermore, this sharp comedown is due to a lack of visibility, when that happens, only the worst-case scenarios are entertained. Growth stocks had a higher valuation because inflation was lower; that is not a bubble. We’ve lived through bubbles, and this is not that. Now there is fear that prices will go higher and that will reignite inflation, but we are not seeing that right now. For me, these are investments, so if it takes to the end of the year, or 12 months from now, a 30% is not shabby at all, even 20% for that matter. So I remain confident that this is the right strategy for me. Once the policy chaos with tariffs calms down, I will widen my target area to include smaller stocks again. I have been investing in a very few smaller-sized stocks, with the vast bulk again being the largest well-known names.

I know that many opinion writers and CIOs of investment organizations are clamoring for earnings estimates to be cut, and that stock valuations are still too high, that they should be at 14 to 15 times. With those calculations, we should trade at 3800 to 4000 on the S&P 500. We are also admonished to ignore the current quarter's earnings since most of it was before this “reciprocal tariffs”. So far, the sample of companies reporting is small, but for the most part, have not disappointed. I believe that this quarter's earnings are just as valid as any, and there was already talk of recession/stagflation in Q1. I am not sure how the financial media is still flogging stagflation when the CPI and PPI for March came in so benignly. So, no, Stagflation is not on the menu, nor is recession; the tariff brouhaha could cause a slowdown. This is because the CEOs of corporations of all sizes have halted investments and commitments until there is more clarity. We haven’t seen any slowdown in employment, and that is the key indicator for recession. Maybe we get down to 1% to 1.5% growth, this is nothing to write home about, but it’s not recession territory.

Trading around a core position to lower my cost basis

Before I get to the heart of the matter, I did find a way to use this yo-yo volatility to our advantage. When Nasdaq jumped 12%, I trimmed off 1% to 3% of the highest-priced shares of each position. Then, when the indexes retreated, I added them back in at a lower price. l would try to get it as close to the cost basis or lower in some cases. Lowering the cost basis raises the overall profit of the investment. I still think that the market has more volatility to go through, so when stocks ended in a rally on Friday, I trimmed a bit more. If we go up straight from here, I am unafraid to hold on to the cash and wait for the next investment opportunity.

I've come to the opinion that Alphabet offers an opportunity for strong capital appreciation

I want to focus on a company and a stock that is underappreciated by market participants and most financial media. Right now, Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL) is my smallest position compared to the five other big-cap names that I have been aggressively accumulating - Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), Nvidia (NVDA), Boeing (BA), and Netflix (NFLX). NFLX reports this week, and if we are lucky, it will sell off hard and we can get some equity on the cheap. Putting that aside, I was inspired toward the end of this week to get bullish on GOOGL by a slew of news items that I read in none other than Seeking Alpha. I believe that GOOGL is borrowing a page from Jeff Bezos’s strategy book.

“Your margin is my opportunity,” Jeff Bezos

The first article that caught my attention was this neutral news piece that didn’t draw any conclusions, as good news reporting shouldn’t. Google in the spotlight as the tech giant signs a deal with the US govt to cut software prices. Quite simply, GOOGL is providing their office software suite of Google Mail, Docs, etc. for 71% off for the GSA as a US Government-wide contract until September. This seems inconsequential since it's a very short-term agreement. That is, if you take that fact at face value, I don’t. I see this as a gambit to take share from Microsoft Office 365. From now until September, it is my opinion that the “DOGE boys” and the GSA will measure user experience and productivity. MSFT maintains a lot of versions of Office, both server and cloud, and Google applications are and always been a cloud service, so it is much simpler to support. Also, in my opinion, Google Mail is more secure than Outlook.

If there is little difference between the two except cost, I would think that Google Office Applications will become the new standard if there is little difference. The heading for this paragraph is the famous saying by Jeff Bezos, “Your margin is my opportunity.” This means, even if GOOGL gets only 29% of what MSFT gets for the same cloud service, it is all pure profit to them. The Google Office bundle is already paid for, so the cost to them is practically nothing, except for cloud services. This is where it gets interesting; much of the value of GOOGL is growth, and a lot of attention is paid to Google Cloud Platform growth. If Google takes the whole contract, that is over 3M seats. That would be 3.6% of the 82.5 million seats Office 365 takes up. In 2024, Microsoft earned $55B for Office 365 products, which is a big margin. The federal government is the largest employer in the US. If it shows big savings without loss of productivity, that would also be a major marketing coup. Other very large organizations that use Office 365 might also want that 31% discount. Microsoft financial numbers for 2024

Google would have to gain more than a trillion in market value to be near the top of the list below. Nabbing the top of the list below will take more than taking market share from Microsoft Office, though it does help.

AAPL market Cap 2.97T

MSFT Market Cap $2.9T

NVDA Market Cap $2.7T

AMZN Market Cap $2.0T

GOOGL Market Cap $1.90T

Waymo is going to be in way more cities

We learned that Waymo is already an operating success in Austin. Again, Seeking Alpha provided me with another interesting item. Waymo has already taken up 20% of Uber Platform’s rides. In this same article, we learned that 22% of rides in San Francisco were served by Waymo, tying Lyft (LYFT) for market share. There was some anecdotal commentary that Waymo is undersupplying cars there, which means they could capture more share. There is good news there as well, Hyundai is going to be manufacturing Waymo Ionic 5s customized with Waymo equipment. Ionic testing to begin later this year, so they should grow their share in San Francisco. Still, Waymo is not sitting on its hands; they are branching out in many major cities in the USA. Waymo robotaxis also operate in Phoenix and Los Angeles. Waymo plans to expand the service to Atlanta, Miami, and Washington, D.C, and they are expanding internationally, starting in Tokyo with mapping city streets.

The projected market is huge. Coherent Market Insights projects the market to reach USD 135.74 billion by 2032, with a CAGR of 64.1% from 2025 to 2032. According to CNBC, Tesla (TSLA) projects the robotaxi market to reach $1.7T by 2040. Speaking of TSLA, they are just getting started in Austin, yet they have been valued largely on their robotaxi potential and robotics. Suffice to say that the market opportunity is huge, and Waymo is way ahead.

TSLA market cap is 1.4T, Toyota Motor (TM) market cap is $221 B. I am being very generous in valuing TSLA's car business and energy storage, and any other revenue to be equal to that of Toyota, arguably the best-run automobile company in the world. So that means that future TSLA is worth about $1.2T as of Friday. I can easily argue that GOOGL should have an equal amount of market value due to it being the leading robotaxi company, and TSLA has hardly gotten started. It should add at least $1T to GOOGL‘s valuation right there. As seen above, TSLA's current cap is $1.9T, add another $1T, and it is right in the running for king of the hill at $2.9T

The elephant in the room, Google Search is losing revenue

With all the negativity about Google Search, I bet you didn’t blink with the above heading stating that revenue was going down. Google Search and other advertising revenue increased by 12% to $54 billion in Q4 2024. I’m not saying that GOOGL’s primacy in search is not being challenged, of course it is, but this is not a difficult nut to crack. GOOGL will offer paid search (blue links), organic search results, and a generative AI solution. There are some indications that advertising growth is slowing, but it is still growing in a slowing economy. Google search is so huge that it does get affected by macro forces. Meanwhile, Google Cloud revenue is profitable and growing 30% a year.

Other growth drivers I didn’t even touch on YouTube being the largest streamer according to MarketWatch. YouTube is the dominant TV streamer and podcaster. In 2024, YouTube advertising rose 14% to $36 billion. Just recently, Alphabet made its biggest acquisition, acquiring Wiz for $35B, an advanced, fast-growing cybersecurity technology that works on multiple clouds, not just GCP. This cybersecurity offering is made for the AI era. It is hard at this point to see what contributions it could make as far as generating revenue. Perhaps the US GSA gave Google applications the opportunity it got because of the Wiz acquisition.

There is no hard and fast formula to arrive at a market cap

One overarching theme is that when a company starts initiating more growth initiatives in rapid succession, the stock gets more attention. The stock market values growth highly, and a company like Alphabet with 2 relatively new businesses, a growing and profitable cloud business, and the fastest growing robotaxi business in the US, in my opinion, should elevate GOOGL to the top of the heap. I am a buyer of GOOGL, and I could see GOOGL approach 260+ per share in 18 months if it gets the recognition it deserves, and proves that it can hold on to the Google Search. GOOGL is an excellent long-term investment with strong potential to be revalued higher once all the new initiatives are recognized. How did I arrive at 260 per share? I took the comparable valuation from TSLA and added $1T to the value of GOOGL. I am not taking anything away from TSLA, I am just saying that if TSLA gets that valuation, so does GOOGL. I added the other growth drivers like the growth of YouTube, the renewed push to take market share from MSFT Office, the very important notion that GOOGL will fix search and integrate generative AI into it. Wiz is a wild card; they have to keep it compatible with MSFT Azure and AWS, which means it is seen as a stand-alone revenue generator. Can it steal share from Palo Alto Networks (PANW) or CrowdStrike (CRWD), perhaps, perhaps not. The Cybersecurity market has a lot of players, and if each gives up a little, Wiz could make a lot.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: IDEXX Laboratories: I Love The Business, Not The Stock Price
data.description:
Introduction

IDEXX's (NASDAQ:IDXX) stated vision for the company is,

To be a great company that creates exceptional long-term value for our customers, employees, and shareholders by enhancing the health and well-being of pets, people, and livestock.

The company aims to achieve this vision through six avenues, as shown below.

The Company's Revenue Drivers

IDXX reportable revenue has three segments, the primary one is CAG or Companion Animal Group, "a reporting segment that provides veterinarians diagnostic products and services and information management solutions that enhance the health and well-being of pets" (2024 10K).

Other than CAG, IDXX also reports revenue from Water quality products, and Livestock, Poultry and Dairy (LPD). The Other operating segment is very small, making up only 0.4% of IDXX's total revenue, so I will not be discussing that.

Five Reasons Why I Like The Business

One: Strong Recurring Revenue

I love a business with recurring revenue.

See the second-largest segment below, called "Consumables"?

Consumables make up 36% of IDXX's CAG sales in 2024, up from 35% in 2023, a segment that boasts the second-largest growth rate of 9.7%. Strictly speaking, consumables are not recurring revenue in the subscription-model sense of the word, i.e. customers are not contractually obligated to buy these consumables. IDXX does not include "consumables" under recurring revenue (more on that later). However, once IDXX's clients buy IDXX's analyzers, they have no choice but to purchase IDXX proprietary consumables frequently and predictably. And that is why I consider this stream of income as "recurring".

Let's illustrate this with one example, IDXX's Catalyst One Chemistry Analyzer, a clinic's on-site laboratory for dogs, cats, exotic animals, horses and livestock.

(This link brings you to the official operation manual, while this link brings you to a video explanation. The description below is a simplified, reconstructed version based on the video and the manual.)

Say a vet wants to find do a routine health check on a pet dog's kidney function. The vet will need a blood sample from the animal. Collecting the blood requires a syringe, a needle, and a lithium heparinized blood separator. Next, a chemistry CLIP (with preloaded set of catalyst slides) is needed to be inserted into the Catalyst One together with the blood. After that, a SDMA slide (Symmetric Dimethylarginine) and SDMA reagent are loaded into the Catalyst One. All the vet needs to do now is to press the start button, and 10 min to 15 min is all it takes for the Catalyst One analyzer to process the blood sample and display the results on the analyzer, and then subsequently transfer that data into the IDEXX VetLab Station, a touch screen, centralized hub that comes bundled with the Catalyst One (and other IDEXX analyzers) used for managing and organizing diagnostic data across the different IDEXX analyzers. On a monthly basis, the vet will conduct a calibration of Catalyst One, and to perform that the vet will need to procure IDXX's Catalyst SmartQC Solution.

All the consumables mentioned above (syringe, needle, lithium heparinized blood separator, SDMA slide and reagent, SmartQC Solution) are not meant to be reused, so new ones have to be purchased from IDXX for the duration of use for Catalyst One.

And IDXX sells other analyzers, not just Catalyst One, each with its set of proprietary consumables, so once a consumer buys an analyzer, the company benefits from recurring revenues from consumables.

How would the data be collected across these different analyzers? Regardless of the analyzers used, the data is consolidated across different IDXX's analyzers for each patient, making the documenting and referencing of patient records easy. This streamlined process makes it easy for the vet to collect data electronically rather than manually, reducing errors.

Then, there is the smaller segment called "recurring revenue", under Veterinary software, services and diagnostic imaging systems. This is the software part. There are two software options for customers to choose from. There is ezyVet, a cloud-based practice management software that improves team productivity and streamlines patient care. There is also Neo Software, a cloud-based software with an intuitive dashboard to help users manage daily activity, manage appointments, get a clear overview of overdue invoices, reminders due and draft invoices, has a one-click feature to access complete patient history, and much more.

Although this portion of the total revenue is not the largest, just 7% of the CAG's revenue segment, but its growth is the highest at 16.7%. Without this recurring revenue portion, CAG's total revenue growth will fall from 6.6% to 5.9%.

Over time, as the number of users expands, the software segment will also grow and will become a significant contributor to IDXX's overall revenue and profit (profit margin is the highest for software), which can pull up the overall results from any lag in sales growth of the hardware.

Two: Sticky Business Model

I love business with a sticky business model.

As described in the Catalyst One example above, it is easy to imagine that once IDXX's customers start to use the products, software, training services, build up their contacts with their clients, and consolidated the patients' medical history in one easy-to-access screen, it can be difficult, costly, and time-consuming to switch to a different service provider. It is sticky.

This is why CEO Jay Mazelsky was confident in declaring at Q4 2024 earnings call,

Our partnership approach has yielded exceptional customer retention rates of over 97%, ensuring that practices continue to see IDEXX as an indispensable partner in their growth and efficiency goals. Customers trust IDEXX to help them navigate challenges of staffing and visit trends.

IDXX's sales team works well with the customers, providing them with excellent support, and is able to sell to new customers and upsell to many existing customers. CEO Mazelsky continued,

IDEXX commercial teams continue to operate at a high-level this year, supporting new business gains, solid net price realization, high-90%s customer retention levels across major testing modalities, and sustained adoption levels of diagnostic testing utilization near post-pandemic highs. High placement levels across our core premium instrument platforms drove a 9% growth in our premium instrument installed base.

The following note from Morningstar analyst Debbie S. Wang is illuminating,

Beyond these technology-based intangibles, Idexx’s major secret weapon lies in its sales representatives and field organization that provide high-touch service to animal hospitals. Idexx reps have proven themselves successful in providing counsel on optimizing hospital operations and improving the profit profile for vets. We have yet to see any other animal health firms carve out this sort of integrated role in animal hospital operations.

Three: Strong Tailwinds

I love business that operates in an industry bolstered by strong tailwinds.

According to data from CapitalOneShopping, the US pet industry revenue has been growing steadily, more than doubled since 2016.

Pet owners are willing to spend on their pets, and the total vet-related expenditure (surgical vet + routine vet visit) comes to a huge 42%.

Four: Predictable Revenue

I love business with predictable and growing revenue.

Over the past twenty years, IDXX managed to grow revenue every year, through the Great Financial Crisis, the crash in 2018, COVID-19, and the bear market from 2022 to 2023. FactSet analysts are projecting continual growth moving forward, and with 100% accuracy rate for their 1-year and 2-year sales forecast, this is as predictable as one can get.

And revenue is not just growing in the United States but internationally as well. CEO Mazelsky shared at the Q4 2024 earnings call that,

Our international regions showcased strong performance in 2024, contributing significantly to our growth story. Double-digit expansion of our premium instrument installed base outside the US underscored the benefits of our targeted global commercial investments over the past three years. Our maturing international sales organization has not only secured high-quality placements but also driven consistent diagnostic utilization gains, particularly in the Europe and Asia-Pacific regions, where we continue to see untapped potential. Spotlighting our European region's very strong performance, we saw the seventh consecutive quarter of double-digit CAG Diagnostics recurring revenue growth, with IDEXX VetLab consumables growth in the high-teens.

Five: Innovation Led Growth Strategy

IDXX spends an increasing amount on R&D every year.

The amount spent in the last five years ($968 million) increased by 72% to the amount spent in the previous five years ($561 million). That helped the company come up with new products and grew the revenue by 59%, from $9.966 billion in 2015 to $$16.849 billion in 2024.

One example of the latest product is the IDEXX inVue Dx Cellular Analyzer that provides "automated, real-time cytology and blood morphology provide results with reference laboratory-level accuracy, within 10 minutes", a new tool that saves vets precious time. CEO Mazelsky says this tool is well received,

The launch of IDEXX inVue Dx has been one of the most exciting developments in 2024, marking a new era in Point-of-Care diagnostics...

Customer interest for IDEXX inVue Dx continues to be exceptionally strong, with nearly 1,600 preorders globally by the end of Q4, reflecting the high demand for this next-generation platform. International interest has been similarly robust as we began taking orders in select geographies in Q4...

... we initiated a controlled launch of IDEXX inVue Dx in Q4 and, to ensure a best-in-class customer experience, we will gradually ramp up placements through 2025...

Customers are simply amazed with how easy the analyzer is to use, eliminating the need for complex slide preparation or interpretation, with results in minutes.

Underpinning the financial guidance Andrew shared is a placement target for 4,500-plus inVue placements over the course of the year, as we march towards our 20,000 placement goal over five years.

Four Risks To Keep Tabs On

One: Declining Growth Rate

Although the average growth rate for the past twenty years was an impressive 13.33%, that declined to 8.03% for the past three years. The forward growth for FY 2025 is expected to be even lower, at just 6.36%.

FactSet analysts have repeatedly lowered their sales forecast for IDXX over the past 6 months for FY 2025, 2026 and 2027. CFO Brian McKeon explained the declining growth rate,

We continue to work through pressure on US same-store clinical visit levels, which declined nearly 3% in Q4 and 2% for the full year in 2024. This has been the primary constraint on IDEXX's recent growth, reflecting transitional sector macro impacts following the accelerated expansion of pet healthcare during the pandemic.

Basically, IDXX has tough comps after the blistering 14% and 19% growth rate for 2020 and 2021 respectively pulled forward a lot of sales. Another reason would be macro headwinds, such as fears of a global economic downturn or recession. The effects of tariffs increasing the cost of goods will be touched on next.

Two: China Risk

Senior VP Andrew Emerson acknowledged negative impacts from tariffs,

We're planning for solid gross margin gains on a comparable basis in 2025, supported by growth in CAG Diagnostics recurring revenues, benefits from lab productivity initiatives, and expansion of our high-margin cloud-based software business. This is partially offset by unfavorable business mix planned from higher levels of CAG Diagnostics instrument revenue gains and preliminary estimates for tariff risk on internationally-sourced materials.

The earnings call was held on February 2, 2025, so these estimates could not have factored in the chain of events that happened since then.

You can read more about these events in my previous article (link here).

The China risk is real, but it is minimal. Not only is the revenue segment from China small to begin with, just 0.92% in 2024, that small amount represents a 27% decline from the 1.46% in 2022.

What is unknown now is the cost impact of the materials sourced from China. Due to the stickiness of the business model, it is likely that IDXX can pass the increased costs to its existing customers. It will be harder to make the case for new potential customers, to convince them to switch to IDXX's analyzers in these uncertain times. It will also be harder to upsell and cross-sell to existing customers. Management warns of this in the 2024 10K,

... additional tariffs on imported goods may increase our costs to purchase products, components, and materials. We may not be able to offset these higher costs through productivity initiatives and price increases, which may materially and adversely affect our business, results of operations, and financial condition. Any price increases we may impose may lead to declines in sales volume or loss of business...

Three: Efficiency Risks

Although revenue has been growing steadily over the years, and management has shown itself astute in its acquisitions (e.g. ezyVet is already part of the product line up) without burgeoning the debt load (long-term-debt-to-capital ratio is only at 21%; gross leverage of about 0.7x at the end of 2024 which is down from 1.3x at the end of FY 2022), there are areas of concern.

I will highlight a few.

First, the cash conversion cycle has increased from 82 days in 2020 to 108 days in 2024, signalling customers have been taking longer time to pay. This slows down the conversion of cash from sales.

Secondly, the days inventory outstanding increased from 65 days in 2020 to 91 days in 2024, indicating a slowdown in sales.

Thirdly, both ROE (return on equity) and ROIC (return on invested capital) have been on the decline since 2024. The deleveraging over the years largely caused the ROE to fall. I am less concerned with this, as I feel more assured when a company is more financially sound.

I am more concerned about the decreasing ROIC figures, as it shows how effectively a company generates profit from its total invested capital, and this is something I will keep an eye on. To be clear, IDXX's ROIC is way higher than some of its peers like Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO) (8.14%) and ResMed (RMD) (20.87%).

Four: Capital Allocation Risk

Management reduced shares outstanding by 13% from 93 million shares to 82 million shares between 2014 and 2024. Furthermore, management stated plans to purchase another $1.5 billion worth of shares in 2025.

Management’s continued emphasis on share buybacks may suggest a lack of opportunities to redeploy the cash internally or through M&A. While I like shareholder friendly actions like share buybacks and I agree that can be an effective way to return excess capital opportunistically when shares are undervalued.

At current valuation levels, management can consider a more conservative way to allocate capital, taking a page from Warren Buffett's playbook to build a cash reserve in short-term Treasuries, or even start a small dividend. These alternatives to share buybacks could provide management with greater long-term flexibility while signaling to shareholders their confidence.

Valuation

IDXX did well in 2024. Earnings per share increased 6% to $10.67. The company's 2025 earnings per share growth outlook is $11.74 per share to $12.24 per share, an increase of 8% to 12%.

Management's initial full-year earnings per share guidance of $11.74 - $12.24 reflects confidence for solid organic revenue growth. Again, note that the guidance was given on February 3, 2025, before Liberation Day.

FactSet analysts have been lowering their adjusted operating earnings over the past six months. Considering the effects of tariffs, it is fair to lower the projections further.

IDXX has an excellent record of besting analysts' 1-year forecast (45%) and 2-year forecast (36%), and meeting their 1-year forecast (45%) and 2-year forecast (64%), showing the reliability of IDXX's ability to generate consistent and predictable earnings.

Noting that IDXX's adjusted operating earning suffered during recessionary periods as well in the form of lower growth (6% growth in 2009), and with the increasing possibility of recession in 2025 and US GDP slowing down, I adjusted bear-case scenario growth rate down to just 3% in 2025, followed by 4% in 2026 and 2027, increased the possibility of my bear-case scenario happening to 30%. I derived at a $397.80 fair value for IDXX, ranging from $249.71 (36% downside) at my low range to $545.43 (39% upside) in my bull scenario. At the current price of $390.94, I do not see a margin of safety nor a favorable risk-reward-ratio. I believe I have been generous in my valuation as I continued to assign a high multiple (ranging from 20 to 30) for a company that is projected to grow at an average low double-digits growth rate for the next few years, deciding that the quality of the company deserves this premium even in the face of potential recession.

Conclusion

I love many things about IDEXX Laboratories. It is a business that is supported by strong tailwinds, has a sticky business model with predictable, recurring revenue, all supported by an innovation-led growth strategy. New products like IDEXX inVue Dx Cellular Analyzer will be supporting new growth both in the US as well as in international markets from 2025.

At the same time, there are some concerns that as an investor, I would want to be cognizant of, like the way management is allocating capital, the tariff risks involving China, and a declining growth rate.

My main concern will be the current valuation, and as someone who would consider an investment only when I see at least a 10% margin of safety, I have to reluctantly put this on my watchlist.
